result.markdown:
# Linear Regression Example: Results & Discussion {#sec-linear-results} ::: {.callout-note} ## About This Chapter This chapter demonstrates **Parts 4--6** of the linear regression project (Descriptive Statistics, Results, and Discussion). This is the main analysis chapter --- the heart of the project. Every code block runs on the PIAAC Korea dataset and produces real output, so you can follow along step by step. If you copy code into your own project, start from the complete script in @sec-r-script or the appendix templates; isolated chunks may depend on earlier objects such as `analysis_df`, fitted models, helper functions, and `ber640_colors`. ::: ## Part 4: Descriptive Analysis {#sec-linear-ex-part4} ::: {.callout-important} ## Guideline Reminder --- Part 4 Before running any regressions, describe your data. Report the sample size, present summary statistics for all continuous variables (mean, SD, min, max) and frequency tables for categorical variables, and include exploratory visualizations that preview the relationships you will model. ::: ### Sample Overview {#sec-linear-ex-sample-overview} We begin by creating the same complete-case analysis dataset used for the descriptives, figures, and regression models. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} analysis_vars <- c( "ln_inc", "female", "schyrs", "schyrs_c", "exper", "exper_c", "exper2_c", "occupation", "numeracy" ) analysis_df <- readRDS(here::here("data", "piaac_kor.rds")) |> mutate(occupation = fct_relevel(occupation, "Skilled")) |> drop_na(all_of(analysis_vars)) tibble( final_n = nrow(analysis_df), analysis_variables = length(analysis_vars), missing_analysis_values = sum(is.na(analysis_df[analysis_vars])), occupation_reference = levels(analysis_df$occupation)[1] ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| final_n| analysis_variables| missing_analysis_values|occupation_reference | |-------:|------------------:|-----------------------:|:--------------------| | 1357| 9| 0|Skilled |
::: ::: The analytic dataset contains **N = 1,357** Korean workers and has complete data on all variables retained for this analysis. The age and employment-status restrictions were applied during data preparation. We use this same `analysis_df` for every descriptive table, figure, and regression model in the chapter. ### Continuous Variables {#sec-linear-ex-continuous} @tbl-desc-continuous presents summary statistics for the four key continuous variables: log-income, years of education, work experience, and numeracy. ::: {#tbl-desc-continuous .cell tbl-cap='Descriptive Statistics for Continuous Variables'} ```{.r .cell-code} continuous_summary <- analysis_df |> summarise( across( c(ln_inc, schyrs, exper, numeracy), list(Mean = mean, SD = sd, Min = min, Max = max), .names = "{.col}__{.fn}" ) ) |> pivot_longer( everything(), names_to = c("Variable", ".value"), names_sep = "__" ) |> mutate( Variable = dplyr::recode( Variable, ln_inc = "Log monthly income", schyrs = "Years of education", exper = "Work experience (years)", numeracy = "Numeracy (z-score)" ) ) |> round_numeric(digits = 2) kable(continuous_summary, align = "lrrrr") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} |Variable | Mean| SD| Min| Max| |:-----------------------|-----:|----:|-----:|-----:| |Log monthly income | 8.00| 0.62| 5.62| 10.44| |Years of education | 13.65| 2.94| 6.00| 22.00| |Work experience (years) | 16.46| 7.73| 0.00| 40.00| |Numeracy (z-score) | 0.00| 1.00| -3.77| 3.04| ::: ::: Several features of the continuous variable distributions are worth noting: - **Log-income** has a mean of about 8.00 and ranges from 5.62 to 10.44. The distribution is approximately symmetric after the log transformation, which is desirable for linear regression. - **Years of education** averages approximately 13.7 years, reflecting Korea's high educational attainment. The range (6--22 years) shows meaningful variation. - **Work experience** averages about 16.5 years. The range (0--40 years) is wide enough to make the curved experience pattern worth checking. - **Numeracy** is z-scored (mean $\approx$ 0, SD $\approx$ 1), ranging from about $-3.8$ to $+3.0$ standard deviations. ### Categorical Variables {#sec-linear-ex-categorical} @tbl-desc-categorical presents the frequency distributions of gender and occupational classification. ::: {#tbl-desc-categorical .cell tbl-cap='Frequency Distributions of Categorical Variables'} ```{.r .cell-code} gender_summary <- analysis_df |> mutate( Category = factor( if_else(female == 1, "Female", "Male"), levels = c("Male", "Female") ) ) |> count(Category, name = "n") |> mutate( Variable = "Gender", Percent = 100 * n / sum(n) ) occupation_summary <- analysis_df |> count(occupation, name = "n") |> mutate( Variable = "Occupation", Category = as.character(occupation), Percent = 100 * n / sum(n) ) |> select(Variable, Category, n, Percent) bind_rows(gender_summary, occupation_summary) |> select(Variable, Category, n, Percent) |> round_numeric(digits = 1) |> kable(align = "llrr") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} |Variable |Category | n| Percent| |:----------|:----------|---:|-------:| |Gender |Male | 814| 60.0| |Gender |Female | 543| 40.0| |Occupation |Skilled | 438| 32.3| |Occupation |Semi-white | 487| 35.9| |Occupation |Semi-blue | 290| 21.4| |Occupation |Elementary | 142| 10.5| ::: ::: The sample is about 60% male and 40% female. The occupational distribution shows that skilled occupations (managers, professionals, technicians) and semi-white-collar workers (clerical, service/sales) are the most common categories, together accounting for about 68% of the sample. Semi-blue-collar workers make up about 21%, and elementary occupations represent about 10% of the sample. ### Visualizations {#sec-linear-ex-visualizations} #### Distribution of the Outcome Variable @fig-hist-lninc shows the distribution of log monthly income, our outcome variable. The log transformation produces a roughly symmetric outcome distribution, which is appropriate for linear modeling. Residual normality is assessed later using model diagnostics. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(analysis_df, aes(x = ln_inc)) + geom_histogram( bins = 30, fill = ber640_colors["accent"], color = "white", alpha = 0.8 ) + geom_vline( xintercept = mean(analysis_df$ln_inc), linetype = "dashed", color = ber640_colors["secondary"], linewidth = 0.8 ) + labs( x = "Log monthly income", y = "Count", title = "Distribution of Log Monthly Income", subtitle = "Dashed line = sample mean" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Distribution of Log Monthly Income](ch05-linear-results_files/figure-html/fig-hist-lninc-1.png){#fig-hist-lninc fig-alt='Histogram of log monthly income centered around 8.0 with a dashed vertical line at the sample mean.' width=2100} ::: ::: #### Scatterplots of Continuous Predictors @fig-scatter-predictors displays the bivariate relationships between log-income and each continuous predictor. Each panel includes both a linear fit and a LOESS smooth to help preview whether a straight-line term is a reasonable starting point. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} scatter_df <- analysis_df |> select(ln_inc, schyrs, exper, numeracy) |> pivot_longer( cols = c(schyrs, exper, numeracy), names_to = "predictor", values_to = "value" ) |> mutate( predictor = dplyr::recode( predictor, schyrs = "Years of education", exper = "Work experience (years)", numeracy = "Numeracy (z-score)" ), predictor = factor( predictor, levels = c("Years of education", "Work experience (years)", "Numeracy (z-score)") ) ) ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x = value, y = ln_inc)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.16, color = ber640_colors["primary"]) + geom_smooth( method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, se = FALSE, color = ber640_colors["secondary"], linewidth = 0.9 ) + geom_smooth( method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, se = FALSE, color = ber640_colors["accent"], linewidth = 0.9, linetype = "dashed" ) + facet_wrap(~ predictor, scales = "free_x") + labs( x = NULL, y = "Log monthly income", caption = "Solid line = linear fit; dashed line = LOESS smooth." ) + theme(plot.caption = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Scatterplots of Log Income vs. Continuous Predictors](ch05-linear-results_files/figure-html/fig-scatter-predictors-1.png){#fig-scatter-predictors fig-alt='Three-panel scatterplot showing log income versus education, experience, and numeracy with linear and LOESS smooths.' width=2100} ::: ::: Education and numeracy both show positive associations with log-income. The relationships are broadly linear in the middle of the distributions, though the LOESS smooths show mild curvature at the tails. Experience shows a positive but concave pattern, consistent with diminishing returns at higher experience levels. #### Boxplots by Categorical Predictors @fig-boxplot-categorical displays the distribution of log-income across gender and occupational categories. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} boxplot_df <- analysis_df |> mutate( Gender = if_else(female == 1, "Female", "Male"), Occupation = as.character(occupation) ) |> select(ln_inc, Gender, Occupation) |> pivot_longer( cols = c(Gender, Occupation), names_to = "predictor", values_to = "group" ) |> mutate( group = factor( group, levels = c("Male", "Female", "Skilled", "Semi-white", "Semi-blue", "Elementary") ) ) ggplot(boxplot_df, aes(x = group, y = ln_inc)) + geom_boxplot(fill = ber640_colors["accent"], alpha = 0.75) + facet_wrap(~ predictor, scales = "free_x") + scale_x_discrete(labels = \(x) stringr::str_wrap(x, width = 10)) + labs(x = NULL, y = "Log monthly income") + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Log Income by Gender and Occupation](ch05-linear-results_files/figure-html/fig-boxplot-categorical-1.png){#fig-boxplot-categorical fig-alt='Two-panel boxplot showing log income by gender and occupation.' width=2100} ::: ::: Two patterns stand out: - **Gender gap:** Male workers have noticeably higher log-income than female workers. The median for males is above the 75th percentile for females. - **Occupation differences:** Skilled workers have the highest log-income and elementary workers have the lowest. Semi-white-collar and semi-blue-collar workers occupy the middle of the distribution and overlap substantially. ::: {.callout-tip} ## Writing Tip Exploratory visualizations serve two purposes: (1) they preview the relationships you will model, and (2) they help you check whether the linearity assumption is reasonable *before* fitting the regression. Always describe what you see in each plot --- do not just drop in a figure without interpretation. ::: ## Part 5: Regression Analysis {#sec-linear-ex-part5} ::: {.callout-important} ## Guideline Reminder --- Part 5 Fit your planned models, present the results in a publication-style table, interpret the focal results substantively, and check regression assumptions. Link each model back to the research question it addresses. Interpret every non-intercept coefficient in the final model, and summarize key coefficient changes across earlier nested models. ::: ### Fit the Planned Model Sequence {#sec-linear-ex-fit-sequence} We fit the same three nested models described in the proposal. All three models use the same complete-case `analysis_df`, so model comparisons are based on the same $N = 1{,}357$ rows. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model1 <- lm(ln_inc ~ female + schyrs_c + exper_c + exper2_c, data = analysis_df) model2 <- lm(ln_inc ~ female + schyrs_c + exper_c + exper2_c + occupation + numeracy, data = analysis_df) model3 <- lm(ln_inc ~ female + schyrs_c + exper_c + exper2_c + occupation + numeracy + female:schyrs_c + female:numeracy, data = analysis_df) ``` ::: ### Model 1: Traditional Mincer Model (RQ1) {#sec-linear-ex-results-m1} Model 1 regresses log-income on gender, years of education, work experience, and the squared experience term. This model answers **RQ1**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_params(model1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Parameter | Coefficient| SE| CI| CI_low| CI_high| t| df_error| p| |:-----------|-----------:|---------:|----:|----------:|----------:|----------:|--------:|--------:| |(Intercept) | 8.2037442| 0.0200473| 0.95| 8.1644171| 8.2430714| 409.220005| 1352| 0.00e+00| |female | -0.3996202| 0.0284360| 0.95| -0.4554037| -0.3438367| -14.053316| 1352| 0.00e+00| |schyrs_c | 0.0837997| 0.0046418| 0.95| 0.0746939| 0.0929055| 18.053434| 1352| 0.00e+00| |exper_c | 0.1687675| 0.0179784| 0.95| 0.1334990| 0.2040360| 9.387260| 1352| 0.00e+00| |exper2_c | -0.0728658| 0.0178090| 0.95| -0.1078021| -0.0379295| -4.091514| 1352| 4.54e-05|
::: ::: **Interpretation of Model 1:** - **Gender:** The coefficient for `female` is $b = -0.400$ ($p < .001$). Among workers with the same education and experience values, the expected monthly income for women is about 33% lower than for men because $100 \times [\exp(-0.400) - 1] \approx -33\%$. - **Education:** The coefficient for `schyrs_c` is $b = 0.084$ ($p < .001$). Each additional year of education is associated with 0.084 higher log monthly income, holding gender and experience constant. On the original income scale, the exact percent difference is $100 \times [\exp(0.084) - 1] \approx 8.8\%$; the shortcut $100b$ gives the close approximation of 8.4%. - **Experience:** The coefficient for `exper_c` is positive ($b = 0.169$, $p < .001$), while the squared term is negative ($b = -0.073$, $p < .001$). This pattern is consistent with a curved association: income increases with experience, but the increase becomes smaller at higher experience levels. - **Model fit:** Model 1 explains about 39.5% of the variance in log-income ($R^2 = .395$). ### Model 2: Adding Occupation and Numeracy (RQ2) {#sec-linear-ex-results-m2} Model 2 extends Model 1 by adding occupation and numeracy proficiency. Because `occupation` is a factor, R automatically compares `Semi-white`, `Semi-blue`, and `Elementary` workers with the `Skilled` reference group. This model answers **RQ2**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_params(model2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Parameter | Coefficient| SE| CI| CI_low| CI_high| t| df_error| p| |:--------------------|-----------:|---------:|----:|----------:|----------:|----------:|--------:|---------:| |(Intercept) | 8.3609839| 0.0301351| 0.95| 8.3018671| 8.4201007| 277.449992| 1348| 0.0000000| |female | -0.4273773| 0.0290611| 0.95| -0.4843872| -0.3703674| -14.706172| 1348| 0.0000000| |schyrs_c | 0.0482662| 0.0058461| 0.95| 0.0367977| 0.0597346| 8.256110| 1348| 0.0000000| |exper_c | 0.1503890| 0.0176656| 0.95| 0.1157339| 0.1850442| 8.513079| 1348| 0.0000000| |exper2_c | -0.0670906| 0.0172596| 0.95| -0.1009492| -0.0332320| -3.887148| 1348| 0.0001064| |occupationSemi-white | -0.1427493| 0.0327123| 0.95| -0.2069218| -0.0785767| -4.363779| 1348| 0.0000138| |occupationSemi-blue | -0.2754407| 0.0412986| 0.95| -0.3564571| -0.1944243| -6.669501| 1348| 0.0000000| |occupationElementary | -0.3773834| 0.0517820| 0.95| -0.4789655| -0.2758013| -7.287926| 1348| 0.0000000| |numeracy | 0.0609567| 0.0150817| 0.95| 0.0313705| 0.0905430| 4.041757| 1348| 0.0000561|
::: ::: **Interpretation of Model 2:** - **Occupation:** Relative to skilled workers, expected monthly income is about 13% lower for semi-white-collar workers ($b = -0.143$), about 24% lower for semi-blue-collar workers ($b = -0.275$), and about 31% lower for elementary workers ($b = -0.377$), holding the other predictors constant. All three differences are statistically significant at $\alpha = .05$. - **Numeracy:** The numeracy coefficient is $b = 0.061$ ($p < .001$). A one-unit increase in numeracy, roughly a one-SD increase, is associated with 0.061 higher log monthly income after controlling for education, experience, gender, and occupation. On the original income scale, this is $100 \times [\exp(0.061) - 1] \approx 6.3\%$ higher monthly income; the $100b$ approximation is 6.1%. - **Education:** The education coefficient decreases from $0.084$ in Model 1 to $0.048$ in Model 2. This suggests that part of the baseline education-income association is shared with occupation and numeracy. - **Model fit:** Model 2 explains about 43.5% of the variance in log-income ($R^2 = .435$), about 4 percentage points more than Model 1. #### Incremental F-Test: Model 1 vs. Model 2 We formally test whether the four additional predictors (three occupation factor contrasts plus numeracy) significantly improve model fit using an incremental $F$-test. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} anova(model1, model2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| Res.Df| RSS| Df| Sum of Sq| F| Pr(>F)| |------:|--------:|--:|---------:|--------:|------:| | 1352| 310.9462| NA| NA| NA| NA| | 1348| 290.2858| 4| 20.66039| 23.98515| 0|
::: ::: The $F$-test is highly significant ($F(4, 1348) = 23.99$, $p < .001$), confirming that occupation and numeracy jointly add significant explanatory power beyond the baseline human-capital variables. This answers **RQ2** in the affirmative: occupation and numeracy improve the explanation of monthly earnings. ### Model 3: Gender Interactions (RQ3) {#sec-linear-ex-results-m3} Model 3 extends Model 2 by adding interaction terms between gender and two key predictors: education and numeracy. These interaction terms test whether the education-income and numeracy-income associations differ between men and women. This model answers **RQ3**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_params(model3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Parameter | Coefficient| SE| CI| CI_low| CI_high| t| df_error| p| |:--------------------|-----------:|---------:|----:|----------:|----------:|----------:|--------:|---------:| |(Intercept) | 8.3517248| 0.0301839| 0.95| 8.2925121| 8.4109375| 276.694336| 1346| 0.0000000| |female | -0.4256092| 0.0289894| 0.95| -0.4824786| -0.3687398| -14.681525| 1346| 0.0000000| |schyrs_c | 0.0554644| 0.0075648| 0.95| 0.0406242| 0.0703045| 7.331863| 1346| 0.0000000| |exper_c | 0.1590659| 0.0178190| 0.95| 0.1241099| 0.1940218| 8.926777| 1346| 0.0000000| |exper2_c | -0.0677306| 0.0172126| 0.95| -0.1014970| -0.0339643| -3.934956| 1346| 0.0000875| |occupationSemi-white | -0.1450277| 0.0326345| 0.95| -0.2090477| -0.0810076| -4.443996| 1346| 0.0000096| |occupationSemi-blue | -0.2632237| 0.0413640| 0.95| -0.3443685| -0.1820788| -6.363596| 1346| 0.0000000| |occupationElementary | -0.3856619| 0.0517703| 0.95| -0.4872213| -0.2841026| -7.449477| 1346| 0.0000000| |numeracy | 0.0804691| 0.0194814| 0.95| 0.0422519| 0.1186862| 4.130567| 1346| 0.0000384| |female:schyrs_c | -0.0145565| 0.0103476| 0.95| -0.0348557| 0.0057427| -1.406747| 1346| 0.1597331| |female:numeracy | -0.0554716| 0.0303910| 0.95| -0.1150905| 0.0041474| -1.825261| 1346| 0.0681832|
::: ::: Because Model 3 includes interactions, the lower-order coefficients are conditional. The `female` coefficient compares women with men when education and numeracy are at their centered/zero values, experience is held constant, and occupation is at the skilled reference category. The `schyrs_c` and `numeracy` coefficients describe the education-income and numeracy-income associations for men, because men are the gender reference group. **Interpretation of the interaction terms:** - **Female $\times$ Education ($b = -0.015$, $p = .160$):** The interaction is not statistically significant at $\alpha = .05$. We do not have clear evidence that the education-income association differs by gender. - **Female $\times$ Numeracy ($b = -0.055$, $p = .068$):** This interaction is close to, but does not reach, the conventional .05 significance level. The estimate suggests the numeracy-income association may be smaller for women than for men, but the individual coefficient is not strong enough for a firm conclusion. - **$R^2 = .439$:** The addition of two interaction terms produces only a small increase in $R^2$ (from .435 to .439). #### Adjusted Predictions for the Gender-by-Numeracy Pattern Interaction coefficients are easier to understand when we convert them into predicted values. `ggeffects::predict_response()` lets us do this with one short command. Here we hold education and experience at their centered values, set the occupation reference category to skilled workers, and compare men and women across selected numeracy values. Because the model includes a squared experience term, the prediction profile must keep `exper_c` and `exper2_c` coherent. Here, setting both to 0 represents average experience because `exper2_c` is the squared centered experience term. ::: {#tbl-numeracy-gender-predictions .cell tbl-cap='Adjusted predicted log-income by gender and numeracy.'} ```{.r .cell-code} numeracy_gender_predictions <- ggeffects::predict_response( model3, terms = c("numeracy [-2,-1,0,1,2]", "female"), condition = list( schyrs_c = 0, exper_c = 0, exper2_c = 0, occupation = "Skilled" ) ) |> as_tibble() |> transmute( numeracy = x, Gender = factor( group, levels = c("0", "1"), labels = c("Male", "Female") ), predicted_log_income = predicted, conf.low, conf.high ) numeracy_gender_predictions |> round_numeric(digits = 3) |> kable(align = "llrrr") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} |numeracy |Gender | predicted_log_income| conf.low| conf.high| |:--------|:------|--------------------:|--------:|---------:| |-2 |Male | 8.191| 8.089| 8.293| |-2 |Female | 7.876| 7.767| 7.985| |-1 |Male | 8.271| 8.197| 8.345| |-1 |Female | 7.901| 7.826| 7.976| |0 |Male | 8.352| 8.293| 8.411| |0 |Female | 7.926| 7.866| 7.986| |1 |Male | 8.432| 8.365| 8.499| |1 |Female | 7.951| 7.875| 8.028| |2 |Male | 8.513| 8.421| 8.604| |2 |Female | 7.976| 7.865| 8.087| ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot( numeracy_gender_predictions, aes(x = numeracy, y = predicted_log_income, color = Gender, fill = Gender) ) + geom_ribbon( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.15, color = NA ) + geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) + geom_point(size = 2.4) + scale_color_manual(values = c( "Male" = unname(ber640_colors["primary"]), "Female" = unname(ber640_colors["secondary"]) )) + scale_fill_manual(values = c( "Male" = unname(ber640_colors["primary"]), "Female" = unname(ber640_colors["secondary"]) )) + labs( x = "Numeracy (z-score)", y = "Adjusted predicted log monthly income", color = "Gender", fill = "Gender" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Adjusted predicted log-income by numeracy and gender.](ch05-linear-results_files/figure-html/fig-numeracy-gender-predictions-1.png){#fig-numeracy-gender-predictions fig-alt='Line plot of adjusted predicted log-income across five numeracy values for men and women. Both lines increase with numeracy, and the male line is higher than the female line across the displayed range.' width=2400} ::: ::: @tbl-numeracy-gender-predictions and @fig-numeracy-gender-predictions show that predicted log-income is higher for men than for women across the displayed numeracy range. The male line rises more steeply: among skilled workers with average education and experience, predicted log-income increases from about 8.19 to 8.51 as numeracy moves from $-2$ to $+2$, while the female prediction increases from about 7.88 to 7.98. This pattern is consistent with the negative `female:numeracy` interaction. Because the individual interaction term is only marginal by conventional standards ($p = .068$), we describe this as suggestive rather than definitive evidence of gender moderation. #### Incremental F-Test: Model 2 vs. Model 3 ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} anova(model2, model3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| Res.Df| RSS| Df| Sum of Sq| F| Pr(>F)| |------:|--------:|--:|---------:|--------:|---------:| | 1348| 290.2858| NA| NA| NA| NA| | 1346| 288.0723| 2| 2.213532| 5.171295| 0.0057906|
::: ::: The incremental $F$-test for the two interaction terms yields $F(2, 1346) = 5.17$, $p = .006$. Although the block is statistically significant at $\alpha = .05$, neither individual interaction term reaches significance on its own. This is a case where the *joint* test detects a small combined improvement in model fit, while the individual terms do not identify a clear single source of gender moderation. For **RQ3**, we conclude that there is limited evidence of gender moderation, but the substantive pattern is still dominated by the large gender level difference in earnings. ### Model Fit Summary {#sec-linear-ex-fit-summary} `compare_models()` gives a quick easystats comparison of the planned model sequence. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} compare_models(model1, model2, model3, rank = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Name |Model | AIC| AIC_wt| BIC| BIC_wt| R2| R2_adjusted| RMSE| |:----|:-----|--------:|---------:|--------:|---------:|---------:|-----------:|---------:| |..1 |lm | 1863.580| 0.0000000| 1894.858| 0.0000000| 0.3945361| 0.3927448| 0.4786882| |..2 |lm | 1778.281| 0.0394062| 1830.411| 0.8828193| 0.4347653| 0.4314108| 0.4625120| |..3 |lm | 1771.893| 0.9605938| 1834.450| 0.1171807| 0.4390754| 0.4349081| 0.4607452|
::: ::: @tbl-linear-fit-summary summarizes the same fit statistics in a compact table. ::: {#tbl-linear-fit-summary .cell tbl-cap='Model fit statistics for the nested linear regression models.'} ```{.r .cell-code} fit_summary <- bind_rows( model_fit(model1) |> as_tibble() |> mutate(Model = "Model 1"), model_fit(model2) |> as_tibble() |> mutate(Model = "Model 2"), model_fit(model3) |> as_tibble() |> mutate(Model = "Model 3") ) |> select(Model, R2, R2_adjusted, RMSE, AIC, BIC) |> round_numeric(digits = 3) kable(fit_summary, align = "lrrrrr") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} |Model | R2| R2_adjusted| RMSE| AIC| BIC| |:-------|-----:|-----------:|-----:|--------:|--------:| |Model 1 | 0.395| 0.393| 0.479| 1863.580| 1894.858| |Model 2 | 0.435| 0.431| 0.463| 1778.281| 1830.411| |Model 3 | 0.439| 0.435| 0.461| 1771.893| 1834.450| ::: ::: Model 2 is a clear improvement over Model 1. Model 3 adds a small amount of fit relative to Model 2, but the practical gain is modest. ### Publication-Style Regression Table {#sec-linear-ex-reg-table} @tbl-regression-models presents all three models side by side in a publication-ready format. For this side-by-side comparison, `modelsummary()` is a compact way to control row labels and keep the nested models aligned. ::: {#tbl-regression-models .cell tbl-cap='Nested linear regression models predicting log monthly income.'} ```{.r .cell-code} model_list <- list( "Model 1" = model1, "Model 2" = model2, "Model 3" = model3 ) coef_map <- c( "(Intercept)" = "Intercept", "female" = "Female", "schyrs_c" = "Education (years, centered)", "exper_c" = "Experience (decades, centered)", "exper2_c" = "Experience squared", "occupationSemi-white" = "Occupation: Semi-white vs Skilled", "occupationSemi-blue" = "Occupation: Semi-blue vs Skilled", "occupationElementary" = "Occupation: Elementary vs Skilled", "numeracy" = "Numeracy (z-score)", "female:schyrs_c" = "Female x education", "female:numeracy" = "Female x numeracy" ) modelsummary::modelsummary( model_list, coef_map = coef_map, gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared", "adj.r.squared", "rmse"), statistic = c("SE = {std.error}", "95% CI [{conf.low}, {conf.high}]", "p = {p.value}"), fmt = 3, output = "markdown" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} standard way to present nested models. It allows the reader to see at a glance how coefficients change as new predictors are added. Always include confidence intervals and standard errors, and include p-values or another clear indication of statistical evidence. ::: ### Collinearity Diagnostics {#sec-linear-ex-vif} We check for multicollinearity using `performance::check_collinearity()` for the full model (Model 3). ::: {#tbl-vif .cell tbl-cap='Variance Inflation Factors for Model 3'} ```{.r .cell-code} collinearity_table <- performance::check_collinearity(model3, ci = FALSE) |> as_tibble() |> select( Term, VIF, `SE inflation factor` = SE_factor, Tolerance ) |> round_numeric(digits = 2) kable(collinearity_table, align = "lrrr") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} |Term | VIF| SE inflation factor| Tolerance| |:---------------|----:|-------------------:|---------:| |female | 1.28| 1.13| 0.78| |schyrs_c | 3.13| 1.77| 0.32| |exper_c | 1.20| 1.10| 0.83| |exper2_c | 1.07| 1.04| 0.93| |occupation | 1.75| 1.10| 0.57| |numeracy | 2.42| 1.56| 0.41| |female:schyrs_c | 2.52| 1.59| 0.40| |female:numeracy | 2.26| 1.50| 0.44| ::: ::: @tbl-vif shows that all VIF values are well below 5 (the highest is about 3.1 for `schyrs_c`), indicating that multicollinearity is not a concern in this analysis. Even the interaction terms (`female:schyrs_c`, `female:numeracy`) have modest VIFs, which is expected because we used mean-centered predictors. ::: {.callout-note} ## VIF Guidelines - VIF < 5: Usually not concerning - VIF 5--10: Conservative flag for discussion; inspect whether coefficients are unstable or redundant - VIF > 10: Stronger warning; affected coefficients may be difficult to interpret ::: ### Residual Diagnostics {#sec-linear-ex-residuals} We assess the regression assumptions by examining residual plots for Model 3 (the most complex model). #### Histogram and Q--Q Plot of Residuals ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} resid_df <- broom::augment(model3, data = analysis_df) |> mutate( case_id = row_number(), cook_threshold = 4 / n(), cook_flag = .cooksd > cook_threshold, large_std_resid = abs(.std.resid) > 3 ) p_hist <- ggplot(resid_df, aes(x = .resid)) + geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = ber640_colors["accent"], color = "white", alpha = 0.8) + labs(x = "Residuals", y = "Count", title = "Histogram of Residuals") p_qq <- ggplot(resid_df, aes(sample = .std.resid)) + stat_qq(alpha = 0.3, color = ber640_colors["primary"]) + stat_qq_line(color = ber640_colors["secondary"], linewidth = 0.8) + labs(x = "Theoretical quantiles", y = "Standardized residuals", title = "Q-Q Plot") p_hist + p_qq ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Normality Assessment of Residuals (Model 3)](ch05-linear-results_files/figure-html/fig-residual-hist-qq-1.png){#fig-residual-hist-qq fig-alt='Two-panel diagnostic display. The histogram shows the distribution of model residuals, and the Q-Q plot compares standardized residuals with theoretical normal quantiles.' width=2100} ::: ::: @fig-residual-hist-qq shows that the residual histogram is approximately symmetric and bell-shaped, with slight heavy tails. The Q--Q plot shows that most points follow the diagonal line closely, with some deviation in the tails. This is typical for earnings data and does not pose a serious threat to inference given the large sample size ($N = 1{,}357$). #### Residuals vs. Fitted Values ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(resid_df, aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.15, color = ber640_colors["primary"]) + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = ber640_colors["secondary"]) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = ber640_colors["accent"], linewidth = 0.8) + labs( x = "Fitted values", y = "Residuals", title = "Residuals vs. Fitted Values", subtitle = "Checking linearity and homoscedasticity" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Residuals vs. Fitted Values (Model 3)](ch05-linear-results_files/figure-html/fig-residual-fitted-1.png){#fig-residual-fitted fig-alt='Scatterplot of residuals versus fitted values with a horizontal zero line and a LOESS smooth.' width=2100} ::: ::: @fig-residual-fitted shows no obvious systematic pattern in the residuals against fitted values. The LOESS smooth is approximately flat around zero, suggesting that the linearity assumption is reasonable. The spread of residuals is roughly constant across fitted values, supporting the homoscedasticity assumption. #### Influence and Outlier Screening `broom::augment()` also gives us leverage, standardized residuals, and Cook's distance. These values help us decide whether a small number of cases might be driving the fitted model. ::: {#tbl-influence-summary .cell tbl-cap='Influence and residual screening summary for Model 3.'} ```{.r .cell-code} influence_summary <- resid_df |> summarise( `Cook threshold (4/n)` = first(cook_threshold), `Cases above 4/n` = sum(cook_flag), `|Standardized residual| > 3` = sum(large_std_resid), `Maximum Cook's D` = max(.cooksd) ) |> round_numeric(digits = 3) kable(influence_summary, align = "rrrr") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} | Cook threshold (4/n)| Cases above 4/n| |Standardized residual| > 3| Maximum Cook's D| |--------------------:|---------------:|-------------------------------------:|----------------:| | 0.003| 53| 12| 0.08| ::: ::: ::: {#tbl-influential-cases .cell tbl-cap='Five cases with the largest Cook\'s distance values.'} ```{.r .cell-code} resid_df |> arrange(desc(.cooksd)) |> slice_head(n = 5) |> transmute( Case = case_id, `Fitted value` = .fitted, Residual = .resid, `Std. residual` = .std.resid, `Cook's D` = .cooksd ) |> round_numeric(digits = 3) |> kable(align = "rrrrr") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} | Case| Fitted value| Residual| Std. residual| Cook's D| |----:|------------:|--------:|-------------:|--------:| | 9| 7.217| 3.226| 7.035| 0.080| | 14| 7.199| 1.703| 3.728| 0.032| | 642| 7.969| -1.948| -4.247| 0.028| | 36| 7.255| -0.938| -2.097| 0.028| | 641| 7.473| -1.849| -4.016| 0.014| ::: ::: @tbl-influence-summary summarizes the influence screening rules, and @tbl-influential-cases lists the five largest Cook's distance values. The common $4/n$ rule is a sensitive screening rule, so flagged cases should be inspected rather than automatically deleted. Here, the maximum Cook's distance is only about 0.08, so no single case appears to dominate the regression results. In a real final project, this is where you would inspect the flagged cases, check for data-entry problems, and report whether a sensitivity analysis changed the conclusions. You should not delete observations only because they are inconvenient. #### Heteroscedasticity and Robust-SE Sensitivity The residual plot is the main diagnostic, but `performance` also provides a simple heteroscedasticity check. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} performance::check_heteroscedasticity(model3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` OK: Error variance appears to be homoscedastic (p = 0.251). ``` ::: ::: For this model, the check does not indicate a heteroscedasticity problem. If you are concerned about unequal residual variance, a simple sensitivity check is to compare the main results with HC3 robust standard errors. The table below focuses on the interaction terms because those are the most delicate part of RQ3. ::: {#tbl-hc3-sensitivity .cell tbl-cap='Conventional and HC3 robust standard errors for Model 3 interaction terms.'} ```{.r .cell-code} robust_interaction_check <- bind_rows( model_params(model3) |> as_tibble() |> mutate(SE_type = "Conventional"), model_params(model3, vcov = "HC3") |> as_tibble() |> mutate(SE_type = "HC3 robust") ) |> filter(Parameter %in% c("female:schyrs_c", "female:numeracy")) |> select( `SE type` = SE_type, Parameter, Coefficient, `Std. Error` = SE, `p-value` = p ) |> round_numeric(digits = 3) kable(robust_interaction_check, align = "llrrr") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} |SE type |Parameter | Coefficient| Std. Error| p-value| |:------------|:---------------|-----------:|----------:|-------:| |Conventional |female:schyrs_c | -0.015| 0.010| 0.160| |Conventional |female:numeracy | -0.055| 0.030| 0.068| |HC3 robust |female:schyrs_c | -0.015| 0.011| 0.202| |HC3 robust |female:numeracy | -0.055| 0.030| 0.067| ::: ::: @tbl-hc3-sensitivity shows that the HC3 sensitivity check does not change the substantive conclusion: the education interaction remains weak, and the numeracy interaction remains suggestive rather than definitive. HC3 robust SEs are used here only as a sensitivity check for possible unequal residual variance. They do not address other threats such as omitted variables, nonlinearity, influential observations, or survey-design features. #### Comprehensive Diagnostics with `performance::check_model()` For a comprehensive view, @fig-check-model shows the `performance` package's diagnostic suite. This plot is useful as a dashboard, but the report text should still explain the specific residual, collinearity, influence, and heteroscedasticity checks above. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} performance::check_model( model3, check = c("linearity", "homogeneity", "qq", "outliers") ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Comprehensive Residual Diagnostics (Model 3)](ch05-linear-results_files/figure-html/fig-check-model-1.png){#fig-check-model fig-alt='Four-panel diagnostic suite from the performance package showing residual patterns, residual spread, Q-Q departures, and influence screening.' width=1800} ::: ::: **Overall assessment:** The regression assumptions are broadly adequate for this worked example. The Q--Q plot shows visible tail departures, and several observations are worth screening, but the residual-vs-fitted pattern is reasonably stable, the heteroscedasticity check is not concerning, and no single case appears to dominate the model. These diagnostics support using Model 3 while reporting the limitations honestly. ## Part 6: Discussion {#sec-linear-ex-part6} ::: {.callout-important} ## Guideline Reminder --- Part 6 Summarize your key findings by directly answering each research question, discuss practical significance, note at least 2--3 limitations, and use association language unless the study design supports causal inference. This is where you show that you understand what the numbers mean. ::: ### Summary of Key Findings {#sec-linear-ex-findings} This study examined how education, experience, occupation, numeracy, and gender are associated with monthly earnings among Korean full-time workers using a series of nested linear regression models applied to PIAAC Korea data ($N = 1{,}357$). The results yield three main findings. **RQ1: Education, experience, and earnings.** RQ1 is answered in the affirmative: education is positively associated with earnings, and experience shows a concave association, with earnings rising at a slowing rate. The education association remains practically meaningful after adding occupation and numeracy, but its smaller size suggests that part of the baseline education-income association overlaps with observed skills and occupation. Together, the traditional Mincer variables explain about 39.5% of the variance in log-income. **RQ2: Added value of cognitive skills and occupation.** RQ2 is also answered in the affirmative. Occupational classification and numeracy proficiency add statistically clear explanatory power beyond the traditional Mincer variables ($\Delta R^2 = .040$, $F(4, 1348) = 23.99$, $p < .001$). The practical gain is moderate overall: occupation differences are large, while numeracy adds a smaller adjusted association beyond schooling. In substantive terms, measured numeracy contains earnings-relevant information not captured by years of schooling alone. **RQ3: Gender differences in education and numeracy associations.** RQ3 has a mixed answer. The joint interaction block improves model fit statistically, and the adjusted predictions suggest that the numeracy-income association may be steeper for men than for women. However, the practical gain is small, the individual interaction estimates are weak or suggestive, and the HC3 sensitivity check does not change that conclusion. There is little evidence that the education-income association differs by gender. The clearest gender-related finding is a persistent conditional level difference in earnings, not definitive evidence of different education or numeracy returns. ### The Persistent Gender Gap {#sec-linear-ex-gender-gap} One of the most striking findings is the large and persistent gender gap. The `female` coefficient is around $-0.40$ to $-0.43$ across all three models, corresponding to roughly a 33--35% earnings disadvantage for women compared with men who have the same education, experience, occupation, and cognitive skills. This pattern is consistent with the well-documented gender wage gap in South Korea, which is among the largest in the OECD. The gap is not accounted for by the measured human-capital variables, broad occupation categories, or numeracy scores in this model. Possible unmeasured contributors include discrimination, career interruptions, job tenure, firm or industry sorting, hours, and within-occupation segregation, but this analysis cannot distinguish among those mechanisms. ### Limitations {#sec-linear-ex-limitations} Several limitations should be noted when interpreting these results. 1. **Cross-sectional design.** Because PIAAC is a cross-sectional survey, we cannot establish causal relationships. The associations between education and earnings, for example, may partly reflect selection effects (e.g., more able individuals obtain more education *and* earn more) rather than a true causal return to schooling. 2. **Single-country sample.** Our analysis focuses on South Korea, which has distinctive features (very high educational attainment, large gender wage gap, specific labor market institutions). The findings may not generalize to other countries or cultural contexts. 3. **Teaching subset, survey design, and numeracy simplification.** This worked example analyzes an unweighted PIAAC Korea teaching subset as a complete-case analytic sample. The standard errors, confidence intervals, $F$-tests, and p-values are ordinary model-based quantities; they do not incorporate PIAAC sampling weights, replicate weights, complex-survey variance estimation, or the full set of plausible values for numeracy. Results should therefore be framed as sample associations in the teaching file, not as nationally representative estimates. 4. **Selection bias.** By restricting the sample to full-time workers aged 35--54, we exclude part-time workers, the unemployed, and those outside the labor force. This sample selection may bias our estimates, particularly for the gender gap, because women are disproportionately represented among nonworkers and part-time workers. 5. **Self-reported earnings.** Monthly income in PIAAC is self-reported and may contain measurement error. The log transformation reduces the influence of extremely high earnings values, but it does not eliminate measurement error. 6. **Model diagnostics and sensitivity.** Residual diagnostics showed some tail departures and several cases worth screening. No single case appeared to dominate the model by Cook's distance, and HC3 robust standard errors did not change the RQ3 conclusion. However, these checks do not eliminate the possibility of model misspecification or unmeasured confounding. ### Model Adequacy and Confidence {#sec-linear-ex-confidence} The diagnostics in Part 5 support using Model 3 as a reasonable summary model. VIF values were low, residual plots did not show severe nonlinearity or unequal spread, and no case dominated the model by Cook's distance. At the same time, the Q--Q plot showed visible tail departures and several observations warranted screening. This means the strongest conclusions are the broad, stable patterns: the positive education and numeracy associations, the occupation hierarchy, and the persistent gender level difference. The borderline interaction pattern should be described more cautiously. ### Implications {#sec-linear-ex-implications} Despite these limitations, the findings have practical implications for how we think about education, skills, occupation, and gender inequality: - The numeracy-income association, net of schooling and occupation, suggests that measured skills should be considered alongside years of schooling. However, the cross-sectional model does not show that a numeracy intervention would cause earnings gains. - The persistent adjusted gender earnings gap warrants attention to structural barriers such as workplace practices, caregiving penalties, and discrimination, although this model cannot identify which mechanism is responsible. - The occupational earnings hierarchy shows that occupational location is strongly associated with earnings. Implications for counseling, training, or mobility programs should be framed as hypotheses for further study rather than direct policy effects from this model. ## Submission Checklist {#sec-linear-ex-checklist} ::: {.callout-important} ## Parts 4--6 Submission Checklist Before submitting your Results & Discussion write-up, confirm that you have completed all of the following: - [ ] **Descriptive statistics** for all continuous variables (mean, SD, min, max) and frequency tables for categorical variables - [ ] **Exploratory visualizations** (histograms, scatterplots, boxplots) with written interpretations - [ ] **Regression models** fitted and summarized (show `model_params()` output while learning, and include a publication-style table in the report) - [ ] **Coefficient interpretation** for every predictor --- direction, magnitude, significance, and substantive meaning - [ ] **Model comparison** using $F$-tests or $R^2$ changes to evaluate incremental contributions - [ ] **Adjusted predictions or conditional-effect plots/tables** for interaction terms, interpreted as conditional associations; copy-and-adapt templates are available in @sec-template-linear-pred-table and @sec-template-linear-interaction-plot - [ ] **Assumption diagnostics** including residual plots, VIF, influence/Cook's distance, heteroscedasticity/HC3 sensitivity when relevant, and a brief conclusion about model adequacy; see @sec-template-linear-diagnostics for templates - [ ] **Discussion** that answers each research question directly, discusses practical significance, uses non-causal language for observational results, frames results as sample associations when appropriate, notes limitations, and explains how diagnostics/sensitivity checks affect confidence in the findings - [ ] All code runs without errors (`eval: true` for all code chunks) - [ ] Major report figures and tables are labeled, captioned, and cross-referenced in prose :::
